pmos

Activation & Resurrection — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · PM Tactics — Growth. Activation and resurrection are the two bookends of the user lifecycle: activation is the quality of the inflow (getting a new user to first value), resurrection is the recovery of the outflow (winning a lapsed user back). Both are inputs to the retention output, and the canon treats them very differently. On activation, the field has converged on a three-moment model — setup → aha → habit, sharpened by Elena Verna — where the aha moment (Sean Ellis) is found by mining must-have users and confirmed via the 40% survey, then operationalized as an activation rate against a single milestone (Lenny Rachitsky: brainstorm → regress → experiment). The setup side is a time-to-value problem (Ramli John & Wes Bush), and the surface where it all happens is onboarding (Samuel Hulick: user success, not feature tours). Resurrection is the lifecycle's other end — growth accounting's New / Current / Resurrected / Churned, with dormancy defined by frequency and a resurrection rate — addressed with win-back campaigns and lifecycle messaging. But resurrection is the most contested lever in growth: Andrew Chen and others argue it's expensive, weak, and usually a symptom that the activation upstream is broken. Where the field genuinely disagrees, this primer flags it. Provenance: practitioner-canon synthesis. 16 verified sources.

Why activation and resurrection are the bookends

Retention gets framed as a single problem, but the levers that actually move it sit at the two ends of the user's life with your product. Activation is the front door: the quality of every new cohort entering, decided in the first session or first week. Resurrection is the back door: whether a user who has already lapsed can be brought back into the active base. Everything in the middle — engagement, habit, stickiness — is what compounds between those two doors, but the doors themselves are where most of the controllable retention is won or lost.

The lifecycle view makes the symmetry explicit. Amplitude's growth accounting sorts every user into New, Current, Resurrected, and Churned, and the prescription for each is different: for new users, "how to get users to come back"; for resurrected users, "why users are coming back." Activation governs the New→Current transition; resurrection governs the Churned→Resurrected one. The framework's striking claim is that "churned users are, in fact, the largest percentage of most products' potential user pool" — which is exactly why resurrection is tempting, and, as later sections show, exactly why it's dangerous to over-index on.

The two are not equal in leverage, and the canon's central tension is about which deserves your attention. The leaky-bucket logic in Amplitude's own playbook — "acquisition won't help your business if you constantly lose new users after onboarding" — points back upstream to activation. Resurrection refills the bucket; activation and the early experience decide how fast it drains. This primer treats activation as the deeper, higher-leverage lever and resurrection as the conditional one, while presenting the practitioners who argue resurrection is worth almost nothing at all.

What activation actually means

The single most common mistake is to equate activation with signup. The canon has converged instead on a three-moment model. As the SaaS activation breakdown lays it out: the setup moment covers the account-creation steps that capture the user's job-to-be-done and get them ready; the aha moment is when "the user actually experiences the product's core value"; and the habit moment "defines the expected frequency at which your users keep using your product." Signup sits before all three — a user who has signed up but not set up, let alone reached aha, is not activated in any sense that predicts retention.

Elena Verna states the model in the same terms — activation is "taking a user from signing up to establishing a habit around your core value prop" — and adds the load-bearing constraint: activation "should be predictive of monetization and retention." That predictiveness is the test of whether your chosen activation definition is real or vanity. If users who clear your activation bar don't retain at materially higher rates than those who don't, you've defined the wrong moment. This is why the model insists on all three steps: a metric that stops at setup measures effort, not value, and will not predict anything.

Finding your aha moment

The aha moment is the fulcrum of activation, and the canonical method for finding it comes from Sean Ellis. Rather than wait for retention cohorts to mature, Ellis works from the users who already consider the product essential. His must-have survey asks "How would you feel if you could no longer use [product]?" and treats the point where "around 40% of users who try it... would be 'very disappointed'" as the signal that sustainable growth is possible. The aha-hunting move is to then "learn everything you can about the people who consider your product a 'must have'" — what benefit they get, who they are — and engineer more new users into that same experience.

The Sean Ellis Score formalizes this as (very-disappointed responses / total respondents) × 100, with a crucial sampling rule that teams routinely get wrong: the survey must run on activated users, those who "have used your product enough to understand its value proposition." Surveying tourists who never reached value measures the wrong population and produces a deceptively low score. The score and the aha moment are two views of the same thing — the score quantifies how must-have the experience is; the aha moment is the in-product event that produces it.

The discipline the serious literature attaches to aha-hunting is causal. A milestone correlated with retention is a hypothesis, not a lever — forcing more users to hit it only raises retention if the relationship is causal. The genre of famous milestones ("7 friends," "2,000 messages") is best read as memorable rallying targets discovered this way, not as universal constants you can borrow.

Defining and measuring the activation rate

Once the aha moment is identified, it becomes a metric. Activation rate is "(users who completed the key activation action / total new signups) × 100," where the milestone is "the minimum action that reliably predicts long-term retention and value realization" — created a project and added a task, connected a data source and viewed a report. The compounding case is large: ProdPad cites that "a 25% increase in activation rate can translate to a roughly 34% increase in monthly recurring revenue over 12 months" because activation feeds retention, expansion, and referral downstream.

Lenny Rachitsky's three-step method for choosing the metric is the practitioner standard: brainstorm candidate aha moments, run regression to find where retention inflects, then experiment to confirm that moving the metric moves retention. The payoff is that "improving the activation rate has an outsized impact on total retention," with activated users retaining roughly 2–3x higher than non-activated ones. A counterintuitive nuance worth carrying: a tighter activation bar — one a smaller share of users clear — can predict retention better than a loose one, because a demanding milestone selects for genuine value realization rather than incidental clicks. The instinct to inflate activation rate by lowering the bar is therefore usually self-defeating.

The senior-PM judgment here is to pick exactly one activation milestone and resist the urge to track a basket. A single, retention-predictive milestone aligns the whole team on one number; a basket of "engagement signals" dilutes that focus and reintroduces the vanity problem activation was meant to solve.

The setup moment and time-to-value

If the aha moment is the destination, the setup moment is the road to it, and the metric that governs it is time-to-value. Ramli John, with Wes Bush, in Product-Led Onboarding, makes the central goal to "minimize the time-to-value (TTV)... the amount of time it takes a new customer to realize value from a product," because in product-led businesses without an upfront sale, users abandon quickly when TTV stretches. Their EUREKA framework is a six-step operational loop — establish a cross-functional team, understand desired outcomes, refine success criteria, evaluate the path, keep users engaged with triggers, apply changes iteratively.

The non-obvious counsel is that the shortest possible TTV is not always the goal: "some friction supports successful adoption." Stripping every step out of setup can remove the very configuration that makes the aha moment land — a CRM with no data imported, a collaboration tool with no teammates invited, will reach a hollow aha that doesn't predict retention. The setup moment's job is to capture exactly the inputs the aha moment needs and no more, which is why the model starts setup by capturing the user's job-to-be-done. Good setup is targeted friction, not zero friction.

This reframes a lot of onboarding work. The question is not "how do we make signup faster" but "what is the minimum the user must configure before the core value can possibly land, and how do we get them through exactly that." TTV measured to a real aha — not to first login — is the metric that keeps setup honest.

Onboarding as the activation surface

Onboarding is where setup and aha are actually delivered, and Samuel Hulick — whose teardowns made onboarding a discipline — defines it far more broadly than a welcome tour: "any time there's an opportunity to increase the likelihood that users are successful when trying to adopt your product." The framing puts user accomplishment, not feature coverage, at the center. Users don't want to learn the interface; they want the outcome the product promises, and onboarding's job is to close the gap between account-created and "I get it."

Hulick's tactical preferences follow from that. He favors action-based learning over tooltip tours — "we know what the very first thing that you should be doing is, so jump into this right now" — rather than making users "reverse-engineer what sort of scenario you would be in where that button would be relevant." Borrowing from game design, the ideal is getting "people to gain mastery without even explicitly realizing that they're being taught," and treating empty states not as dead ends but as guided first actions. The throughline is that the best onboarding is indistinguishable from using the product well.

For senior PMs the implication is that onboarding is not a layer you bolt on after the product is built; it is the activation path itself, and it competes for the same engineering effort as features. A product-tour modal is the cheapest, weakest form; designing the first-run experience so the core action is the first action is the expensive, durable one.

The B2B activation wrinkle

The three-moment model was sharpened on self-serve consumer and SMB products, and Elena Verna flags where it strains in B2B. The first error is unit-of-analysis: activation is often mis-measured at the individual-user level when account-level activation is what predicts revenue. A single user reaching aha inside a ten-seat deal does not activate the account; the team has to reach a collaborative threshold before the contract renews. Treating one activated user as an activated account systematically overstates health.

The second error is sequencing, and it is the one Verna is most pointed about: measuring retention on users who have not yet activated. Users still in the setup-to-aha stretch are not retainable in the normal sense — they have not yet realized value, so a "retention" number computed over them blends two different populations and invites the wrong tactics. The corollary is that retention should be measured on activated users, and the activation funnel measured separately on everyone else. This connects directly to resurrection: as a later section shows, a large fraction of "dormant" users in B2B never activated in the first place, which means the right intervention is an activation fix, not a win-back email.

The broader senior-PM lesson is that activation definitions do not transfer across business models. A consumer freemium app, a sales-assisted B2B platform, and a marketplace each have different units (user, account, two-sided match), different aha moments, and different natural frequencies — so borrowing another company's activation metric is as unsafe as borrowing its retention benchmark.

Resurrection and the lifecycle view

Resurrection is the lifecycle's other bookend, and it is best understood through growth accounting. Amplitude casts resurrection as one of three retention levers — new-user activation, current-user engagement, and resurrection — each with its own diagnostic question; for resurrection it is "why users are coming back." The appeal is scale: churned users are "the largest percentage of most products' potential user pool," and the framework asserts "it's cheaper to resurrect a churned user than it is to acquire one," since they already have an account and prior product familiarity.

Amplitude's resurrection playbook (explicitly "part 3" of the three-lever series) gives the operating loop: define dormancy by your own product's usage pattern, discover "resurrected user triggers — the external factors and previous campaigns that brought back some of your dormant users," then route dynamic dormant cohorts to win-back channels. The whole thing sits under the leaky-bucket premise that "acquisition won't help your business if you constantly lose new users after onboarding" — which is the lifecycle framework quietly conceding that the front door (activation) gates the value of the back door (resurrection).

Defining dormancy and the resurrection opportunity

Resurrection is impossible to measure without first defining dormancy, and the definition is product-specific. Amplitude defines a resurrected user as "a previously inactive customer who returns to use your product after a defined dormant period" by performing a qualifying action, and scales the dormancy threshold to natural frequency: roughly "7–14 days" for daily-use apps, "21–28 days" for weekly tools, "45–60 days" for monthly services. Set the window too short and you'll flag healthy sporadic users as dormant; too long and you'll miss the recoverable window entirely. This is the same natural-frequency discipline that governs retention measurement, applied to the back door.

The metric is the resurrection rate = (resurrected users / total dormant users) × 100. The economic case rests on the familiar asymmetry that "acquiring a new customer can cost 5 to 25 times more than retaining an existing one," and the argument that a complete growth strategy "must address all three dimensions: acquisition, retention, and resurrection." The hard prerequisite the same source stresses is operational clarity: success "depends on establishing clear operational definitions" of active, dormant, and resurrected — without them, the resurrection rate is uninterpretable and win-back targeting is blind.

The opportunity is real but bounded. The dormant pool is large, but — as the contrarian section argues — much of it is structurally unrecoverable, and the headline cost-advantage statistics describe retention generally, not the much harder economics of winning back someone who has already left.

Resurrection tactics and win-back

The tactical literature on win-back is consistent, and its first rule is diagnostic, not promotional. Userpilot frames most inactivity as "an activation failure, not a loyalty failure," and splits dormant users into three segments that demand different treatment: never-activated users (re-inviting them to the same experience that lost them underperforms), activated-then-drifted users (the most recoverable, because they "know what good looks like"), and context-changed users (who often self-reactivate when circumstances shift). The single most useful move is specificity over generic appeals — "You completed 70% of setup—let's finish domain verification" beats "come back and see what's new."

Braze rounds out the mechanics: a win-back campaign is "a targeted strategy to re-engage users who have become inactive or are showing signs of churn," triggered after a defined inactivity window (30/60/90 days), requiring granular segments rather than blanket sends, and working best on "those who already know your brand but have drifted away." The recurring caution across both sources is that the population matters more than the message: win-back lands on the activated-then-drifted segment and largely wastes spend on the never-activated, for whom the correct intervention is upstream in onboarding.

The practical synthesis is to segment dormant users by whether they ever activated before doing anything else. That single cut determines whether a user is a win-back candidate (drifted) or an activation-fix candidate (never reached value) or best left alone (context-changed) — and it is the step generic "we miss you" campaigns skip, which is why so many of them fail.

The case against resurrection

Resurrection is the most contested lever in growth, and the strongest skeptic is Andrew Chen. His argument is that needing to "push" users back is itself the diagnosis: it means "your web product has failed to integrate itself in your user base's life, and there isn't a recurring set of traffic that you can depend on." He ranks push/notification-driven return at the bottom of a hierarchy beneath users who come back on their own, and models how a notification-dependent base mathematically collapses — from a thousand users to a small remnant in a cycle — because each push reaches fewer people. The version of resurrection he endorses is organic: engaged users pulling lapsed ones back through natural product usage (a coworker re-inviting someone into a shared tool), not the company bombarding them with reminders.

A complementary critique sharpens the economics: "if you have to beg your users to come back, you've probably already lost them." Resurrection "doesn't scale as a growth strategy" because it traps teams in a leaky bucket, and "every euro and hour spent on resurrection is an euro and hour not spent on improving the product" — the deeper claim being that "retention is a product problem, not a marketing one." Both critiques converge on the same point: if D1 retention is already low, no win-back sequence will bend the curve, because the product hasn't earned the return.

This is a genuine disagreement with the lifecycle-framework camp, not a settled question. The reconciliation most practitioners reach is that resurrection is a real lever only on top of a product that already retains — it harvests recoverable drifted users but cannot manufacture value that was never there. Run before activation is fixed, it is expensive motion that recycles the same users through the same broken door.

Sequencing the two levers

The bookends are not co-equal claims on your roadmap; there is a near-universal sequencing rule. Fix activation first. The case is both mechanical and economic: most dormancy is an "activation failure, not a loyalty failure," so a large share of the dormant pool you might target with win-back never reached value and will re-churn through the same experience. Until the New→Current transition is healthy — until the curve flattens for fresh cohorts — resurrection campaigns recycle users through a door that doesn't hold.

The leaky-bucket framing that even the pro-resurrection sources adopt — "acquisition won't help your business if you constantly lose new users after onboarding" — applies with equal force to resurrection: refilling a draining bucket is motion, not progress. Andrew Chen's collapse math is the quantitative version of the same warning, and the "retention is a product problem" critique is the strategic version. The order is: get activation predictive of retention, confirm the curve flattens for new cohorts, then harvest the genuinely recoverable drifted segment via targeted win-back — and even then, prefer organic resurrection (engaged users pulling others back) over paid nudges.

The senior-PM takeaway is to read a resurrection request as a question about activation. When someone proposes a win-back program, the first diagnostic is whether new cohorts retain; if they don't, the resurrection budget belongs upstream. Resurrection is the lever you earn by fixing activation, not a substitute for it.

Live debates

Several questions in this space are genuinely contested, and a primer should flag rather than resolve them.

  • Is resurrection worth doing at all? The lifecycle-framework camp treats it as one of three co-equal retention levers tapping the "largest potential user pool"; Andrew Chen and the "retention is a product problem" camp argue paid/notification resurrection is weak, expensive, and usually a symptom of broken activation. The reconciliation — resurrection works only atop a product that already retains — is widely held but not universal, and teams genuinely disagree on how much to invest.
  • Correlation vs causation in the aha moment. The famous activation milestones are taught as gospel, but the rigorous method treats any milestone as a hypothesis until an experiment proves that forcing it lifts retention. How much weight to put on a discovered-but-unproven milestone is unsettled.
  • How tight should the activation bar be? The intuitive goal is a high activation rate, but practitioners note a tighter, lower-percentage bar can predict retention better. There's no settled answer on where to set it — it's a per-product empirical question.
  • Is there one aha moment, or several? The single-milestone discipline aligns teams, but multi-product and B2B contexts may have role- or segment-specific aha moments; whether to collapse them into one metric or track several is contested.
  • User vs account activation in B2B. Elena Verna argues account-level activation is what predicts revenue, but many teams still instrument at the user level because it's easier; the right unit of analysis is an active debate as PLG meets enterprise sales motions.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

  • Retention — activation and resurrection are the two inputs whose output is the retention curve; this primer is the lever-level companion to the retention diagnostic.
  • Onboarding & first-run experience — the surface where setup and aha are delivered; Hulick's teardown discipline deserves its own deeper treatment.
  • Product/market fit — Sean Ellis's 40% survey and the flattening retention curve are two PMF tests that activation work is meant to move.
  • Growth loops & the AARRR funnel — activation and resurrection are two named stages; organic resurrection is itself a loop where engaged users pull lapsed ones back.
  • Experimentation & causal inference — the "correlation until proven causal" caveat on aha-hunting is where activation meets experiment design.
  • Lifecycle & CRM messaging — win-back, triggered emails, and dormancy windows are the operational layer beneath resurrection.
pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_activation-and-resurrection.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
